title: What the guys at University of Florida are really like
body:
As a short guy, you gotta own it. Not everyone cares but id be lying if i didn’t say it’s very prevalent. If im out at a bar with friends or whatever you just gotta be confident.

Im not a pick up artist but I’ve have my fun lol. Just be confident about yourself. You don’t need validation from anyone. Be confident, friendly, respectful, and cordial.

You attract people by how you act. That’s how I think. Im not the type to go hit on women or regularly try to pick them up. Every so often when I was single, good times happened.

Always unexpected, always when I was myself and having a good time. Great experiences. Don’t set yourself up for disappointment. People suck and are judgmental. Just have fun and be yourself.

Life is better that way, at least for me. You don’t need to find validation in hoping a women finds you attractive even though your short. You’ll be miserable if you need that validation from anyone but yourself.

I’m happy with who I am. Always have been even when people try to knock you down. Doesn’t matter to me. Im gunna enjoy the ride while I can

title: Last Minute Biology EOC Cram Session // 25min Crash Bio Review!
body:
Heres the AI version of my message😭

I took my Florida EOC today and scored a 5. There were 51 questions on my exam. Here's an overview of the topics covered:

Few questions on Embryology
Many questions on ecology (environment and ecosystems)
1 or 2 questions on energy in ecosystems/energy pyramid
3 or 4 questions on organelles
1 or 2 questions on parts of the brain
1 question on viscosity of blood
Many questions on cells & cell theory
Few questions on cellular respiration and photosynthesis
2 or 3 questions on monomers, polymers & macromolecules (like lipids, amino acids, monosaccharides, carbohydrates, proteins, glucose, starch)
1 question on identifying a macromolecule, monomer, or polymer based on an image
Many questions on evolution
1 question on Punnett squares
2 or 3 questions on genetics & inheritance (other than Punnett square question)
1 or 2 questions on Taxonomy, classification, & domains
1 or 2 questions on codons in relation to DNA & RNA
2 or 3 questions on natural selection & other biological processes
1 question on a food web with a diagram
3 or 4 questions on understanding scientific theories and laws
1 question on microscopes (specifically viewing cells via microscopes)
2 or 3 questions on the carbon cycle & sustainability
Few questions on plants (reproduction, genetics, etc)
1 question on properties of water
Some questions were common sense. It's important to eliminate answers you know are wrong and to read the entire question and answers carefully. Words like "most likely" or "best" in relation to the answer choices may indicate that the answer is not 100% correct but is the most plausible based on scientific principles.

When answering questions, even if you feel like you totally understand it, find key words and define them.

- Mitosis, for example, is a type of cell division that creates body cells in animals (including humans) and is involved in growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction in some organisms. It creates two identical diploid cells with the exact same DNA as the parent cell. During mitosis, cells spend most of their time preparing for division and less time actually dividing. The process involves the stages of prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, followed by cytokinesis;

- Fungi are heterotrophic organisms that have cytoplasm, cell walls, and are eukaryotic. They are decomposers, breaking down organic matter;

- Bacteria can be autotrophic or heterotrophic and most have cell walls. They are prokaryotic, unicellular organisms with ribosomes. Mutations in bacteria can help them develop immunity/resistance against antibiotics, similar to how animals evolve through evolution.

It's important to understand the relationships between concepts in the questions and how to interpret diagrams based on that. A testing strategy that helped me was to eliminate choices I knew were wrong and select the best answer, flagging the question to come back to it later. Going back and double-checking can help clarify any questions you struggled with. MOST IMPORTANTLY READ THE QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS CAEFULLY TO AVOID OVERLOOKING IMPORTANT INFORMATION OR WORDS THAT SWIFT THE MEANING OF THE STATEMENT!

I hope this helps! Good luck to everyone taking the exam.❤
author: @AveryUsoro

body: I´m a quadriplegic from Brazil, I went there many many years ago and it went one of the best days of my life. title: Discovery Cove Orlando FULL TOUR & REVIEW | TIPS
body: Tip for glasses wearers - if you go to the shop you can borrow a free prescription snorkel visor for the day.  This info was pretty buried in the website last year and I only stumbled across it by accident!  My eyesight is shocking and I was worried about how much I'd be able to see underwater without my glasses, but the prescription visor worked a treat.  I'd recommend getting one as early as possible because they obviously only have limited numbers of each prescription strength.

title: Discovery Cove Orlando FULL TOUR & REVIEW | TIPS
body: One of the best way to spend time in Orlando. We have been several times now and love it. Just about everything is included in the price, Sea World runs specials throughout the year and while fun it doesn’t run you down like other theme parks might. We did have the dolphin encounter we purchased and a thunder storm rolled in. After an hour of trying to wait it out we were given a voucher to come back for our encounter. Luckily I also plan a couple of days of rest, like using the hotel amenities or going up the I drive, so we were able to go back. When we did they assigned us a time and told us to just enjoy the park, so we essentially got another whole day with no extra expense. This has become a must do for our family when we visit Orlando.

title: What THEY'RE NOT TELLING YOU About Moving To Florida | Honest Review 5 Years Later
body:
From what I’ve gathered after a few videos…
-don’t live right on the coast but don’t live too far inland either, closer to the panhandle if possible(coast is pricey and lots of people, too far inland is swampy, too far down into Florida gets hotter and hotter)

Tourists were literally hunted in Florida back then.  Rental cars had a distinguishable license plate so Floridians knew who to target.  Besides Tourists, Police officers were randomly shot when pulling over vehicles.  That’s when I learned if I am ever pulled over, to keep my hands on the wheel where the officer can see them, until he asks for my license.  I left and never returned.  I prefer 4 month northern winters to 8 month Florida summers.

title: Florida State University - 10 Things I Wish I Knew Before Attending
body: Omg he isn't lying about Alumni Village not being in the best area. When I lived there when I was a gradate student, there was a crack house literally across the street from the bus stop outside of the village's fence. But I actually never had a problem. Tbh you kinda had to take a nole or city bus to get to school anyway, unless you were okay with walking about 45min to get to your classes. Sadly, the Village in 2014 closed due to the cost of projected renovations. I was heartbroken when the final decision was made because I had fond memories of living there. Quiet and quaint and full of history (they were built after the war to help veterans go back to school and have a place to live with their families). It wasn't fancy by ANY means, but it was sturdy and had the basic necessities...Im a low maintenance gal, so I never had a reason to complain. Plus there was always security there because there were two daycares on campus. Never had an issue with anything popping off around there. It's strange now to drive by and see nothing but a wisp of what used to be....alas. I will be going back for another graduate degree in the fall...I bleed Garnet and Gold. 😎

title: Meet The Most Dangerous Women In America's Prisons | Trevor Mcdonald
body:
Going to prison saved my life. I was a drug addict for 10+ years and in 2019 I went to prison and found out I was pregnant. When I got there that night, the intake nurse told me I was pregnant. Thank god I was arrested when I was... by the time I got to the prisons OB, she informed me I was 8 weeks along. That was 2 weeks after I arrived so I was 6 weeks pregnant when I was arrested. Talk about a blessing in disguise! Prison saved my son's life as well. My son will be 5 this December (2024), I have over 5 years sober and I just started school in August for nursing ❤

Edit: thanks for the support! I just finished my second semester of school!!!!

Edit 2: thanks everyone again! I'm in my second year of school.. finally taking the classes that have more to do with nursing like biology, etc. my son is almost 6!!! He's thriving in kindergarten ❤ I also finished my (as I referred to it as) my addiction puzzle. The last piece for me was quitting cigarettes. It was my one last vice and I've been nicotine free for 4 weeks as of tomorrow (October 17, 2025) woohoo!!

body: For those that don’t know him . Sir Trevor McDonald is a distinguished British journalist and broadcaster, renowned for his tenure as the presenter of ITV’s “News at Ten.” Born on August 16, 1939, in Trinidad and Tobago, he began his career in the Caribbean before relocating to the United Kingdom in 1969. In 1973, he joined Independent Television News (ITN) as a reporter, becoming the first Black news presenter on British national television. Throughout his career, Sir Trevor has been celebrated for his authoritative presence and exceptional speaking abilities. body: Quit blaming the parents, sometimes it is the kid.  We did everything we could for our son, but he had street smarts or should I say he thought he did.  He got in to drugs.  That was his downfall.  We sent him to rehab many times.  I was afraid of him.  He wanted to throw me of a balcony.  Don’t judge somebody’s journey until you have walked in their shoes!
author: @DaisyMom01

title: The Kids Serving 50+ Years For Murder (Prison Documentary) | Our Life
body: The fact that the officer that runs their block cares for them and really tries to be down to earth and understanding that they are human just makes me feel so good for him. He’s such a great person, and you can tell he loves his job and what he does for these young kids that are in these horrible horrific crimes. ❤️
author: @katelynschaefer9947

title: The Kids Serving 50+ Years For Murder (Prison Documentary) | Our Life
body: That young man can SING! I wish nothing but the best for him in life when he gets out, I hope for a full rehabilitation. When he said “He shouldn’t have hit my mama” I felt that.
author: @mzspargo

title: The Kids Serving 50+ Years For Murder (Prison Documentary) | Our Life
body: I lost my mother at 7yrs old, grew up in house hold full of alcohol, drug, physical and psychological abuse, then lost my sister to drugs at 15yrs old, I got kicked out of two schools and was heading down a very violent and dark path, luckily I had my grandfather a former soldier take me in and discipline me to become a good man and use my story to happen for me, instead of to me.  Each of these men just need a good mentor in their lives to inspire them because they're all courageous and can be great people.  It's not too late.

title: Ride-Along With Florida Sheriff (exclusive access) 🇺🇸
body: Lifetime Jax resident here.  This is by far the best piece ever in explaining the operations of JSO.  Local media seems to approach JSO actions with the usual legacy media agenda.  This showed a side of JSO that many did not know even existed, especially the proactive policing components.  Thanks Peter for putting this out there in such a raw and unbiased fashion, and allowing the world to see the extraordinary job Sheriff Waters and his staff perform on a day-to-day basis, and most importantly portraying the City of Jacksonville in such a  positive light.  Also, kudos to Vic Micolucci, who I am sure had a large role in putting this together.  You'll never know the positive impact this one piece will have had on JSO and the City of Jacksonville.  Great job to everyone involved.
author: @jamessavage-y5x

title: The Florida Nobody Knows 🇺🇸
body:
When I was an exchange student living in South Georgia, people would ask me when I got back home how living in the US was. My answer was always "I can only speak on how it was living in South Georgia as the USA is such a vast country with a lot of different cultures". My Host family used to take me down to visit North Florida a lot, and I loved it so much. It's still one of my favorite places 13 years later. Thank you for taking me back! So many fun memories.
xx a Norwegian
